1: Paul shifts from argument to diatribal dialogue — he voices the Jewish reader's counter-objection. The advantage of being Jewish has just been called into question by the argument of Romans 2; the expected pushback is "then what's the point?" This kind of voiced objection is characteristic of the diatribe form; Paul throughout Romans voices objections more sharply than any actual opponent would, then answers them. The question is legitimate and Paul takes it seriously rather than dismissing it.
2: "The Jews have been entrusted with the very words of God" (ta logia tou theou) — the Hebrew scriptures were the supreme Advantage. In an oral-aural culture where texts were rare and literacy limited, possession of a body of sacred writing attributed to the creator of the universe was an extraordinary privilege. The Psalms celebrate this gift: "He has revealed his word to Jacob, his laws and decrees to Israel — he has done this for no other nation" (Psalms 147:19-20). Paul does not deny this advantage but will show in 3:9ff that it did not exempt Israel from the universal indictment.
3: "What if some were unfaithful? Will their unfaithfulness nullify God's faithfulness?" — the diatribe continues. The question touches on a deep theological anxiety in Second Temple Judaism: how do the repeated failures of Israel — the golden calf, the apostasies of the kings, the exile — square with God's unconditional covenant promise to Abraham? Texts like Baruch, 4 Ezra, and the Qumran documents wrestle with this. Paul's answer draws on the logic of the OT itself: God's covenant faithfulness is not contingent on Israel's performance.
4: "Let God be true, and every human being a liar" — Paul cites Psalms 51:4 (LXX Romans 50:6): "that you may be proved right when you speak and prevail when you judge." In its original context, this is David's confession after his sin with Bathsheba — he acknowledges God's righteous judgment against him. Paul extracts the universal principle: in any dispute between God's word and human testimony, God wins. The forensic language ("prove right," "judge") fits the quasi-legal argument Paul is building toward the universal indictment of Romans 3:9-20.
5: The diatribe speeds up through Romans 3:5-8, voicing and answering increasingly absurd objections. "If our unrighteousness brings out God's righteousness more clearly, is God unjust in bringing his wrath on us?" — this was a genuine theological problem in Judaism: if human sin provides the backdrop against which divine grace shines, does God have grounds to punish sin at all? Paul brackets the question with "(I am using a human argument)" — he acknowledges the logic is drawn from ordinary human reasoning, not from revelation, and thus has limited weight.
6: "If that were so, how could God judge the world?" — the argument from divine universality. In Second Temple Jewish theology, God's role as judge of the whole earth was foundational (Genesis 18:25: "Will not the judge of all the earth do right?"). If God's justice were compromised by the logic of Romans 3:5, his role as universal judge would be incoherent. The objection is answered by demolishing the premise, not by explaining how divine justice and human responsibility coexist — that comes in Romans 5-6.
7: A sharper version of the objection in Romans 3:5: if my falsehood enhances God's glory, why am I condemned? This presses the "contrasting backgrounds" logic to its absurd conclusion — individual falsehood as a contribution to divine glory. Paul treats this as a species of rationalization, the kind of argument that makes bad behavior appear virtuous. The implied context is debates within the early church about whether sin "freed up" more grace (explicitly addressed in Romans 6:1-2).
8: "Why not say — as some slanderously claim that we say — 'Let us do evil that good may result?'" — Paul acknowledges that his gospel of grace had been caricatured as moral license by his opponents. This was a real charge against Pauline Christianity: if justification is by faith apart from works, what prevents libertinism? Paul's response here is simply to denounce both the doctrine and those who attributed it to him — the full answer comes in Romans 6-7. The phrase "their condemnation is just" is emphatic and formulaic — a moral verdict delivered without extended argument.
9: "Do we have any advantage? Not at all! For we have already made the charge that Jews and Gentiles alike are all under the power of sin" — Paul ties together the preceding argument. "Under the power of sin" (hyph hamartian, lit. "under sin") — sin is a power, an enslaving dominion, not merely a catalogue of individual acts. This personification of sin as an enslaving ruler was not merely rhetorical; it reflected the ancient understanding of cosmic powers (cf. 1 En 8-9, where angelic figures are responsible for teaching humanity wickedness).
10:
Paul begins a string of OT citations (Romans 3:10-18) drawn from multiple books — Psalms 14:1-3; Psalms 53:1-3; Ecclesiastes 7:20; Psalms 5:9; Psalms 140:3; Psalms 10:7; Isaiah 59:7-8; Psalms 36:1. This technique of stringing texts together by theme was known in rabbinic interpretation as haraz (string of pearls or chain of texts) — a method of demonstrating that a single theological point is multiply attested in scripture. By weaving together texts from the Psalms, wisdom literature, and the prophets, Paul argues that the universal condemnation of humanity is the consistent testimony of the Hebrew canon, not Paul's personal opinion.

"There is no one righteous, not even one" — from Psalms 14:1-3 (= Ps 53), which begins "The fool says in his heart, 'There is no God.'" The Psalm's context is divine observation of universal moral failure. Paul quotes it as authoritative divine speech.
11: "There is no one who understands; there is no one who seeks God" — continuing Psalms 14:2. The Psalm presents God looking down to see "if any understand, if any seek God" — and finding no one. The irony is that the very act of reading Romans is, for the reader, an instance of seeking God — which means the Psalm's sweeping negation is a literary and rhetorical device (consistent with its wisdom-poetry genre) rather than a literal claim about every individual. Paul uses it as a universal indictment of the human condition under sin, not a denial that any individual ever repents.
12: "All have turned away, they have together become worthless; there is no one who does good, not even one" — completing Psalms 14:3 (LXX). "Become worthless" (ēchreiōthēsan) — the word suggests something gone rancid or functionally useless; it is used of spoiled food in some Greek texts. The image is of human moral capacity as corrupted by sin, not merely as failing in individual acts.
13: "Their throats are open graves; their tongues practice deceit. The poison of vipers is on their lips" — from Psalms 5:9 and Psalms 140:3. The anatomy of speech — throat, tongue, lips — is detailed: each organ of communication becomes an instrument of destruction. "Open graves" evokes the most extreme form of ritual uncleanness in Jewish law: contact with a corpse defiles. The throat-as-grave suggests that words flowing from it carry death and pollution.
14: "Their mouths are full of cursing and bitterness" — from Psalms 10:7. In the Psalms' context, this describes the wicked person who oppresses the vulnerable and who thinks God does not see. Paul uses it here as part of the universal picture rather than a description of a specific social type. The cataloguing of sins by the speech organs (Romans 3:13-14) was a recognized form in both Greek rhetoric (descriptions of flatterers, slanderers, demagogues) and Jewish wisdom literature.
15: "Their feet are swift to shed blood" — from Isaiah 59:7-8, a passage that Paul draws on through Romans 3:17. Isaiah 59 is a national lament about the moral collapse of Israel — the prophet says "our iniquities have separated us from our God" (Isaiah 59:2). Paul applies Isaiah's indictment of Israel's sin to the universal human condition, enlarging the scope of an already sweeping text.
16: "Ruin and misery mark their paths" — continuing Isaiah 59:7. The word "ruin" (suntrimma) was used in military contexts for the wreckage left after a siege — a landscape of broken things. The catena's progression moves from the organs of speech (throat, tongue, lips, mouth, Romans 3:13-14) to the organs of action (feet, Romans 3:15-16) — the entire human body is implicated in the testimony of the scriptures against universal human sin.
17: "The way of peace they do not know" — completing Isaiah 59:8. The context in Isaiah is explicitly about the community's failure to maintain covenant justice and righteousness; the consequences are the absence of peace. Shalom in its full sense — the integrated well-being of covenant life — is inaccessible to those who have abandoned the moral structure on which it depends. Paul will return to the theme of peace (eirēnē) extensively in Romans 5:1 (peace with God) and 14-15 (peace within the community).
18: "There is no fear of God before their eyes" — from Psalms 36:1 (LXX Romans 35:2). This verse closes the catena and supplies the theological explanation for all the preceding symptoms: the fear of the Lord is the beginning of wisdom (Proverbs 1:7; Romans 9:10); its absence is the root from which all the catalogued sins grow. In the Hebrew wisdom tradition, "fear of God" was not psychological terror but the orientation of the whole life — cognitive, emotional, moral, practical — toward God's reality and authority.
19: "Whatever the law says, it says to those who are under the law, so that every mouth may be silenced and the whole world held accountable to God" — the rhetorical capstone of the indictment. The OT citations in Romans 3:10-18 were drawn from Israel's own scriptures; if those scriptures condemn Israel, they condemn everyone, since the law's voice reaches first to those within its covenant and by extension to all humanity. "Every mouth silenced" — the forensic image: in a court, when the defendant has no answer to the charge, they fall silent. All humanity is reduced to this silence before the divine tribunal.
20: "No one will be declared righteous in God's sight by the works of the law; rather, through the law we become conscious of our sin" — Paul echoes Psalms 143:2 ("Do not bring your servant into judgment, for no one living is righteous before you"). The law's function, as Paul will develop in Romans 7:7-13, is diagnostic rather than salvific: it reveals sin without resolving it. "Works of the law" (erga nomou) — a phrase that has generated significant modern debate (whether it means Torah observance in general or specifically identity markers like circumcision, food laws, and sabbath). Paul's argument requires the broader sense: no performance of any legal demand achieves the standing the law itself demands.
21:
"But now apart from the law the righteousness of God has been made known" — nyni de (but now) is the great hinge of Romans and arguably of Paul's entire theology. It marks the eschatological turn: a new moment has arrived, distinct from everything that preceded it. "Apart from the law" does not mean "in contradiction to the law" — Romans 3:21b immediately clarifies that the Law and the Prophets themselves testify to this righteousness. The law could not produce the righteousness it demanded; it has now been produced by a different means.

In the Second Temple period, many Jewish writers anticipated a decisive divine intervention that would transform the moral situation of humanity — what Paul is describing in Romans 3:21-26 is that this transformation has arrived in Christ's death and resurrection, though in a form no one had predicted.
22: "This righteousness is given through faith in Jesus Christ to all who believe. There is no difference between Jew and Gentile" — the "no difference" (ou gar estin diastolē) deliberately echoes Romans 2:11 ("God does not show favoritism"). The symmetry is structural: in Romans 2, no difference in accountability; in Romans 3, no difference in access to righteousness. The Gentile-inclusion is not an afterthought but the logical entailment of the universal indictment: if all are under sin equally, and if righteousness comes through faith equally, then ethnicity is irrelevant to covenant standing.
23: "For all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God" — husterountai tēs doxēs tou theou. "Fall short" is a present indicative — an ongoing condition, not merely a past act. "The glory of God" in Jewish thought encompassed both God's radiance (the shekinah of the temple) and the human vocation to image God. Some Second Temple texts (4 Ezra 7:122; 1QS Romans 4:23; CD Romans 3:20) speak of the "glory" as a primordial dignity lost at the fall and to be restored in the new age. Paul may be invoking this tradition: to "fall short of God's glory" is to fall short of what humanity was created to be, not only to fail to reach a distant standard.
24: "Justified freely by his grace through the redemption that came by Christ Jesus" — "redemption" (apolytrōsis) is release-through-payment, the language of manumission (buying a slave's freedom) and of ransom (paying for a prisoner's release). In the LXX it is used for the Exodus — God's great act of redemptive release from slavery in Egypt (Exodus 6:6; Deuteronomy 7:8; Romans 15:15). Paul presents Christ's death as the new Exodus: liberation from the slavery of sin at a cost, paid by God rather than by the enslaved. The word "freely" (dōrean) emphasizes that no contribution is demanded from the enslaved party.
25:
"God presented Christ as a sacrifice of atonement" (hilastērion) — the Greek word hilastērion is used in the LXX as the technical term for the kapporeth, the golden lid of the Ark of the Covenant — the "mercy seat" (Exodus 25:17-22; Leviticus 16:14-15). On Yom Kippur (the Day of Atonement), the high priest entered the Holy of Holies once a year and sprinkled the blood of the sin offering on the mercy seat, making atonement for Israel's accumulated sins. This was the most sacred act in Israel's annual liturgical calendar — hidden from all eyes, performed in secret behind the veil.

Paul's application is striking in its reversals: where the mercy seat was hidden in the Holy of Holies, Christ was "set forth publicly" (proetheto) — displayed in crucifixion for all to see. Where the blood was sprinkled ritually on the golden lid, Christ's blood was shed in historical execution. The one annual sacrifice becomes the once-for-all sacrifice (cf. Hebrews 9:12). Paul's Jewish readers would have recognized the Day of Atonement imagery immediately.

"Because in his forbearance he had left the sins committed beforehand unpunished" — the accumulated sins of the entire OT period, atoned for repeatedly but never finally, are now addressed by the cross. The cross demonstrates that God was never simply overlooking sin; his patience was holding open the space for the definitive atonement that was coming.
26: "To be just and the one who justifies those who have faith in Jesus" — the theological tension Paul resolves: how can God be both perfectly just (requiring that sin be punished) and the one who acquits sinners? The cross is the answer: in Christ, God bore the weight of sin's condemnation himself, so that justification is not a compromise of justice but its satisfaction. This is the logical center of Paul's argument. The Second Temple period offered multiple theories of atonement (martyrological, representative, substitutionary elements appear in 4 Maccabees, the Qumran texts, and apocalyptic literature); Paul synthesizes these in light of Christ's specific historical death.
27: "Where, then, is boasting? It is excluded" (ekkleiō) — the verb means to shut out, to bar from entry. Boasting (kauchēsis) in the Torah and in Jewish identity was not mere arrogance but a form of legitimate covenant confidence — the recognition that being entrusted with God's law was a gift and a calling. Paul does not attack this attitude as such; he argues that the faith-righteousness of Romans 3:21-26 structurally excludes it, because faith is precisely the posture of receiving rather than achieving.
28: "A person is justified by faith apart from the works of the law" — the compressed thesis statement that will dominate Reformation-era debates. "Works of the law" (erga nomou) — whether this means all law-keeping or specifically the ethnic-identity markers (circumcision, food laws, calendar observances) that divided Jew from Gentile is a major scholarly debate. Paul's argument here points toward the broader sense: any form of legal performance, since the law's function is to condemn rather than to justify (Romans 3:20).
29: "Or is God the God of Jews only? Is he not the God of Gentiles too?" — the monotheism argument. Jewish theology was strictly monotheistic (Deuteronomy 6:4, the Shema: "Hear O Israel, the Lord our God, the Lord is one"). One God logically entails one standard, one judge, and one means of right-standing. If God were only the God of Jews, he could have a Jewish-specific method of justification; if he is the God of all humanity, there must be a universal access point. Paul's radical claim is that faith is precisely that universal access point, which is why it makes the gospel available to Gentiles without requiring them to become Jews first.
30: "Since there is only one God, who will justify the circumcised by faith and the uncircumcised through that same faith" — the Shema (Deuteronomy 6:4) becomes the theological foundation for Gentile inclusion. The distinction between "by faith" (ek pisteōs) and "through faith" (dia tēs pisteōs) for circumcised and uncircumcised respectively has generated debate; most interpreters see it as stylistic variation rather than a theological distinction. The point is the unity of the means: one God, one faith, one people — the logical entailment of Jewish monotheism applied to its full scope.
31: "Do we, then, nullify the law by this faith? Not at all! Rather, we uphold the law" (nomon histanomen, lit. "we cause the law to stand") — Paul anticipates the obvious charge that his faith-righteousness abolishes the Torah. His answer is more compressed here than the extended argument of Romans 7; the grounds for "upholding" the law through faith will include: (a) the law itself testified to faith-righteousness (Romans 3:21; the Abraham argument of Romans 4); (b) faith fulfills the law's moral demand through the Spirit (Romans 8:4); (c) the law's diagnostic function is confirmed, not dissolved, by the gospel. This verse serves as a bridge to the Abraham argument that follows in Romans 4.
